message:
feat(fnp-python): passthrough wrappers for 10 core numpy ufuncs (k74v.1)

First slice of the reality-check epic franken_numpy-k74v.1 "70 missing
core numpy ufunc passthrough wrappers". Lands the tutorial-critical
subset so `fnp_python` actually exposes `add`, `sin`, `log`, `sqrt` and
friends under numpy's own names instead of only through existing
aliases like `square`/`cbrt`.

New #[pyfunction]s in crates/fnp-python/src/lib.rs, each one a thin
delegate to `numpy.<name>`:

  Unary ufuncs (7):
  - abs        (registered as py_abs to avoid shadowing Rust's abs)
  - absolute
  - sin, cos
  - log, exp
  - sqrt

  Binary ufuncs (3):
  - add
  - subtract
  - multiply

All ten are added to the module via `m.add_function(wrap_pyfunction!(...
, m)?)?;` immediately after `linalg_matrix_norm`, keeping the existing
registration order intact.

New oracle test in the same file walks the surface end-to-end:

- Calls each unary ufunc on a strictly-positive 1-D array and asserts
  `numpy.allclose(ours, theirs)`.
- For ufuncs defined over negatives (abs/absolute/sin/cos/exp), also
  compares on a mixed-sign array.
- Confirms integer-input dtype-promotion parity via
  `dtype().str()` string equality.
- Verifies `abs` on a Python complex(3, 4) returns the real magnitude
  5.0 on both sides.
- Exercises the binary ufuncs with 1-D arrays, (3,1)x(1,4) broadcasting
  into (3,4), and integer-dtype preservation for `add`/`subtract`/
  `multiply`.
- Spot-checks `log(1) == 0`, `sqrt(0) == 0`, `sqrt(4) == 2` as
  boundary-value sanity.

No Rust-native implementation changes; fnp-ufunc still owns the real
kernels. These are surface-only wrappers so code written against
`numpy` can swap to `fnp_python` at the import line.
